topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official letter lay on the worn oak of my desk, the ink stark against the cream paper. My hands trembled slightly as I traced the lines, each word a deliberate, crushing weight. I reread the section detailing the financial aid—the perfect score, the rigorous application process, the sheer, exhausting effort I’d poured into securing this. Then, the small, almost invisible footnote. It wasn't mine. It belonged to a name I didn't recognize, a name associated with a different lineage, a different need. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I pushed the paper away, the sound a sharp, brittle snap in the quiet room. I stared out the window at the grey campus quad, the sunlight doing nothing to warm the chill that had taken root inside me.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." The words felt thin, reedy, swallowed by the cavernous silence of my dorm room. I kept running my thumb over the edges of the acceptance packet, seeking some misplaced error, some smudge that would prove the whole thing a fabrication. Every time I focused, the realization hit me again: the entire edifice of my academic future was built on sand, a misplaced foundation. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, involuntary surrender to the unfairness of it all. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small rug, the rhythm of my steps uneven and agitated. I wanted to scream, but the sound caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping noise.

[story 3]

I sat at the library table, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead like trapped insects. The documents spread before me felt alien, a puzzle with a missing piece that refused to slot into place. My eyes kept flickering back to the signature line, a stark contrast to the glowing accolades I had chased. A slow, cold wave washed over me, draining the color from the room. I picked up my pen, but my fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive. I started erasing the dates, trying to scrub the truth from existence, but the paper seemed to mock my efforts, refusing to yield. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and just stared at the ceiling, feeling utterly depleted, as if the energy required to keep functioning had simply evaporated.
